l1: Question 1 — When did the issue first start occurring?
Question 2 — Is the disk internal or external to the server?
Question 3 — Does the disk disappear consistently or intermittently?
Question 4 — Is the issue affecting user access or server processes?
Question 5 — Has any recent OS update or patch been applied?
l2: Advanced question 1 — Are multiple disks affected or only one specific disk?
Advanced question 2 — Has the server hardware recently been modified or updated?
Advanced question 3 — Are there any RAID or virtualization layers involved with the disk?
Advanced question 4 — Is the disk part of clustered storage or SAN?
Advanced question 5 — Are there performance degradation patterns before disappearance?

l1: Command or question 1 — Check if the disk appears in Disk Management
Command or question 2 — Verify if the disk is listed in Device Manager
Command or question 3 — Run a basic hardware rescan in Windows
Command or question 4 — Use diskpart list disk to confirm visibility
Command or question 5 — Test with another USB/SATA cable if external
l2: Advanced command 1 — Review Event Viewer logs for disk or controller errors
Advanced command 2 — Run PowerShell Get-Disk to confirm OS detection
Advanced command 3 — Check controller firmware and driver compatibility
Advanced command 4 — Run chkdsk /f /r to scan for corruption
Advanced command 5 — Use vendor diagnostic utilities for SMART health

l1: Resolution step 1 — Re-seat or reconnect the disk cables
Resolution step 2 — Update or reinstall basic disk drivers
Resolution step 3 — Test the disk on another system if possible
Resolution step 4 — Disable USB selective suspend if external
Resolution step 5 — Ensure power settings do not turn off the disk
l2: Advanced fix 1 — Update server BIOS and storage controller firmware
Advanced fix 2 — Run diagnostic tools from the disk manufacturer
Advanced fix 3 — Check RAID configuration or rebuild array if degraded
Advanced fix 4 — Replace faulty controller or backplane if errors persist
Advanced fix 5 — Escalate to vendor support if hardware failure suspected

l1: Export log X — Save Event Viewer system logs
Collect trace Y — Gather Device Manager hardware status
Run command Z — Execute diskpart list disk output
Step 4 — Capture screenshot of Disk Management
Step 5 — Collect OS build and patch level
l2: Advanced collection 1 — Capture performance monitor counters for disk I/O
Advanced collection 2 — Collect storage controller diagnostic logs
Advanced collection 3 — Export full PowerShell storage subsystem report
Advanced collection 4 — Gather SMART data from vendor utilities
Advanced collection 5 — Collect RAID controller logs and config dump

disk_auto_eject.title: Disk auto-ejecting or disappearing from File Explorer

steps.action: Open Disk Management and check if the disk is visible there.
steps.expect: Disk should appear in Disk Management even if not in File Explorer.

steps.action: Check Device Manager for any disk or controller errors (yellow exclamation marks).
steps.expect: Disk should be listed without errors in Device Manager under Disk drives.

steps.action: Review Event Viewer (System log) for Event ID 51, 153, or disk-related errors.
steps.expect: Identify any disk timeout, controller failure, or power state change events.

steps.action: Run: diskpart > list disk to confirm OS-level disk detection.
steps.expect: Disk should be enumerated by diskpart even if File Explorer doesn't show it.

steps.action: Disable USB selective suspend: Control Panel > Power Options > Change plan settings > Change advanced power settings > USB settings > USB selective suspend setting > Disabled.
steps.expect: Disk should remain connected without intermittent disconnections.

steps.action: Update disk and storage controller drivers from Device Manager or vendor website.
steps.expect: Driver update resolves compatibility or stability issues.

steps.action: Monitor the disk in File Explorer and Disk Management for stability over 24 hours.
steps.expect: Disk remains visible and accessible without auto-ejecting.
